# keta — agent guide

keta is a reflection-free, codegen-free HTTP server framework for Dart (SDK
^3.12). Routing, middleware, transactions, lifecycle, and OpenAPI output are
carried by a constructor graph and routes-as-values. User code reads as plain
Dart. This file is enough to write handlers, DTOs, mappers, schema constants,
and tests by hand.

Packages: `keta` (core), `keta_db` + `keta_sqlite` + `keta_rds` (database), `keta_openapi` (Schema + OpenAPI). Dependencies flow inward; `keta` has none beyond the SDK.

## Routing

Two syntaxes converge on one `Path` value.

String syntax — plain handler, parameters read via `c.param`:

```dart
app.get('/health', (c) => c.text('ok'));
app.get('/users/:id', (c) => c.text(c.param<String>('id')));
```

Typed DSL — `app.on(path)` opens the same verbs; the handler receives the captured tuple. Build paths from `root` with `.segments('a/b')` (a `/`-separated run of literals, batched in one call, arity-preserving) and `.capture(...)` (arity-transitioning). Built-in captures are the constants `string`, `integer`, `number`, `boolean`; naming has exactly one form — call the capture — `string('uid')` (there is no separate `named()` helper):

```dart
app.on(root.segments('users').capture(string('uid')).segments('posts').capture(integer('postId')))
   .post((c, (String, int) p) async {
     return c.text('${p.$1}/${p.$2}');
   });
```

A custom capture is a `parse` + `schema` pair: `Capture<T>(T Function(String) parse, {String? name, required Map<String, Object?> schema})`; `parse` must throw `BadRequest` on invalid input (every other exception is a defect → 500).

Verbs: `get post put delete patch head options`. Registering the same
method+template twice is a boot-time `StateError`. Match precedence is literal
over capture. `app.group('/prefix')..use(m)` scopes middleware to a subtree.

## Context

`c.env` (your environment), `c.method`, `c.uri`, `c.routeTemplate` (the matched route's template, e.g. `/users/:id`; `null` when nothing matched) — the bounded-cardinality dimension for metrics, spans, and hand-rolled logging; a raw request path is attacker-controlled and unbounded, so it must never feed a label axis, and there is no `c.route` that falls back to it. `c.header('name')` (lower-cased, first value), `c.headerAll('name')` (every value, multi-value symmetry), `c.remoteAddress`. `c.cookie('name')` / `c.cookies` read request cookies (parsed once from the `Cookie` header, RFC 6265 pair syntax; malformed pairs skipped, first-wins on duplicates, values kept as opaque octets). To set a cookie, render a `SetCookie` value onto ordinary multi-value headers — there is no second channel: `c.json(v, headers: {'set-cookie': [SetCookie('sid', 'abc', maxAge: Duration(hours: 1), httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: SameSite.lax).toHeaderValue()]})`. `SetCookie(name, value, {maxAge, expires, domain, path, secure, httpOnly, sameSite})` validates name/value/domain/path at construction (a `;`, control char, etc. throws `ArgumentError` — header injection is unrepresentable) and enforces that `SameSite.none` requires `secure: true` (RFC 6265bis); `expires` is emitted as an IMF-fixdate in UTC. `c.param<T>('name')` parses a path capture — String/int/double/bool (bad input → 400). Query parameters are fully symmetric with `param`: `c.query<T>('name')` (absence → 400 too — required-ness is expressed by which accessor you call, not by a flag), `c.tryQuery<T>('name')` (absence → null, the optional form), `c.queryAll<T>('name')` (repeated `?tag=a&tag=b` keys; absence → `[]`). `c.get<T>(key)` / `c.tryGet` / `c.set` use identity `Key<T>('name')` (never const). `c.body()` decodes JSON (cached; bad JSON → 400; over the size limit → 413); `c.bodyBytes()` / `c.bodyStream()` for raw. `c.aborted` completes on timeout or disconnect (cooperative). Responses: `c.json(value, {status})`, `c.text(str, {status})` — `status` is named (Dart forbids optional-positional after named). `c.log` is `env.log` with reqId and route baked in — the matched template, or the fixed placeholder `unmatchedRoute` (`'(unmatched)'`, exported by keta) when nothing matched, so even ad hoc logging never keys on the raw path either. `c.routeDoc` is the matched route's opaque `doc` (a keta_openapi `RouteDoc` when present) — the core carries it without interpreting it; `enforceSecurity` (below) is what reads it.

## Errors & middleware

`KetaException` is sealed — everything a handler may throw or a client may receive is one named subtype, each `const Xxx(message, [detail])`: `BadRequest` (400, the canonical parse/validation failure), `Unauthorized` (401), `Forbidden` (403), `NotFound` (404), `Conflict` (409), `PayloadTooLarge` (413, `maxBodyBytes` exceeded), `UnprocessableEntity` (422), `NotImplementedYet` (501), `Unavailable` (503), `GatewayTimeout` (504, `timeout()`). For a status with no named subtype, use the factory `KetaException.status(status, message, [detail])`. The one-sentence rule: throw a `KetaException` subtype and the response carries its status; every other exception (`ArgumentError`, `StateError`, `FormatException`, ...) is a defect → 500, logged, no detail leaked. A non-removable last-resort fallback converts any uncaught error just before the transport: `KetaException` → its status + `{"error": message}`, anything else → 500 + empty body + logged. `recover()` is the customizable form of the same — being sealed, an exhaustive `switch` works when you override it. Built-in middleware: `accessLog()`, `recover()`, `cors(allowOrigins: [...])`, `etag()`, `gzip({threshold: 1024})`, `timeout(Duration)`, `tracing()`. Apply with `app.use(...)`; they run in registration order, app-wide before group. `etag()` adds a strong `ETag` (FNV-1a 64 over the buffered body — no crypto dep; a cache validator needs a fast fingerprint, not collision resistance) to 200s and answers a matching conditional `GET`/`HEAD` with `304` (weak comparison, `*`, and comma lists per RFC 9110; content headers dropped; stream bodies and non-200s pass through). `gzip()` compresses a buffered `String`/`List<int>` body when the request accepts gzip (honoring `gzip;q=0`), adds `Content-Encoding: gzip` and `Vary: Accept-Encoding`, and skips compression for streams, already-encoded bodies, 204/304, or bodies under `threshold`; the transport then frames the correct post-compression `Content-Length`. Recommended ordering when both are used: register `gzip()` before `etag()` (gzip outer, etag inner) so the tag is computed over the un-encoded body — deterministic regardless of the compressor (RFC 9110 §8.8.3 permits a pre-encoding validator).

## Canonical forms (write these exactly)

Scope: primitives (String/int/double/bool), `T?` (optional, omitted from
`required` and from `toJson` when null), `List<T>`, nested DTOs (`$ref` + deps),
enums (plain form: values are `name` strings; the enhanced wire form below when a
wire value isn't a plain identifier), and sealed types (oneOf + discriminator).
Generics are out of scope.

The complete DTO shape — hand-written, agent-written, and lint-materialized code
must be identical:

```dart
enum Role { admin, member }

class UserDto {
  final String id;
  final String name;
  final int? age;
  final Role role;
  final List<String> tags;
  UserDto({required this.id, required this.name, this.age,
      required this.role, required this.tags});

  factory UserDto.fromJson(Map<String, Object?> json) => UserDto(
        id: json['id'] as String,
        name: json['name'] as String,
        age: json['age'] as int?,
        role: Role.values.byName(json['role'] as String),
        tags: (json['tags'] as List).cast<String>(),
      );

  Map<String, Object?> toJson() => {
        'id': id,
        'name': name,
        if (age != null) 'age': age,
        'role': role.name,
        'tags': tags,
      };
}

const userDtoSchema = Schema('UserDto', {
  'type': 'object',
  'required': ['id', 'name', 'role', 'tags'],
  'properties': {
    'id': {'type': 'string'},
    'name': {'type': 'string'},
    'age': {'type': 'integer'},
    'role': {'type': 'string', 'enum': ['admin', 'member']},
    'tags': {'type': 'array', 'items': {'type': 'string'}},
  },
});
```

Nested DTO: in `fromJson`, `Other.fromJson(json['x'] as Map<String, Object?>)`;
in the schema, `'x': {'\$ref': '#/components/schemas/Other'}` and
`deps: [otherSchema]`.

Optional `factory X.fromRow(Map<String, Object?> row)` builds from a DB row —
same shape as `fromJson`, but numeric-origin values are converted explicitly.

Sealed with a discriminator — `switch` on the `type` field (lowerCamel variant
names); schema is `oneOf` + `discriminator`, each variant an ordinary DTO:

```dart
sealed class Event {
  factory Event.fromJson(Map<String, Object?> json) => switch (json['type']) {
        'created' => Created.fromJson(json),
        'deleted' => Deleted.fromJson(json),
        _ => throw const BadRequest('unknown event type'),
      };
  Map<String, Object?> toJson();
}
```

Enhanced enum — when a wire value is not a plain Dart identifier (a hyphen, a
leading digit) or is a reserved word, the enum carries the wire string. The Dart
name is derived deterministically (already-valid → verbatim; else split on
non-alphanumerics and camel-join, `super-user`→`superUser`; leading digit → `$2fa`;
reserved word → `default_`). The Schema lists the WIRE strings, never the Dart
names. A plain enum (all wire values already identifiers) stays `enum Role { admin,
member }` with `Role.values.byName(...)`/`.name`:

```dart
enum Role {
  admin('admin'),
  superUser('super-user');
  const Role(this.wire);
  final String wire;
  static Role fromWire(String wire) => values.firstWhere(
        (v) => v.wire == wire,
        orElse: () => throw BadRequest('unknown Role wire value: $wire'),
      );
}

const roleSchema = Schema('Role', {'type': 'string', 'enum': ['admin', 'super-user']});
```

Field mappers use `Role.fromWire(json['role'] as String)` / `role.wire` (scalar) and
`(json['roles'] as List).map((e) => Role.fromWire(e as String)).toList()` /
`roles.map((e) => e.wire).toList()` (list). `fromWire` throws `BadRequest`, so a file
with an enhanced enum imports `package:keta/keta.dart`.

A single `Schema` constant is one source of truth: OpenAPI output,
`schema.validate(value)` (list of violations) and `schema.require(value)`
(throws `BadRequest` with the violation list as `detail` on any violation),
and contract tests.

Usage: `final dto = UserDto.fromJson(userDtoSchema.requireMap(await c.body()));`
(`requireMap` requires, then hands the result back typed as `Map<String, Object?>` — the shape every write handler needs before `fromJson`. `require` remains the untyped primitive — `Object? require(Object?)`, unchanged — for a schema that isn't `type: object`.)

## List endpoints

Every list endpoint hand-writes the same three things: pagination that clamps instead of rejecting, a `{items, total}` response, and a Schema for that envelope. `listSchema(itemSchema)` (from `keta_openapi`) canonicalizes only the third — it is a helper over the writing pattern, not a generic or codegen, so the handler and the response map below are still written by hand, exactly like every other route:

```dart
app.get(
  '/users',
  (c) async {
    // Clamp both bounds rather than trust them: an unbounded limit lets one
    // request scan the whole table, and a negative bound is a SQL error
    // waiting to happen. An offset past the end is an empty page with the
    // right total, not a 400 — the honest answer for a paging UI that
    // overshoots the last page.
    final limit = (c.tryQuery<int>('limit') ?? 20).clamp(1, 100);
    final offset = (c.tryQuery<int>('offset') ?? 0).clamp(0, 1 << 31);
    final rows = await c.env.db.reader.query(
      'select id, name, age, role, tags from users order by id limit ? offset ?',
      [limit, offset],
    );
    // total counts the whole set, independent of limit/offset.
    final total = await c.env.db.reader.query('select count(*) as n from users');
    return c.json({
      'items': [for (final r in rows) UserDto.fromRow(r).toJson()],
      'total': total.first['n'] as int,
    });
  },
  // Not const: listSchema builds a new Schema per call (it interpolates
  // itemSchema.name), so a RouteDoc that embeds it cannot be const the way a
  // hand-written const Schema's RouteDoc can.
  doc: RouteDoc(
    success: Success(schema: listSchema(userDtoSchema)),
    summary: 'List users',
    query: [QueryParam('limit', integer), QueryParam('offset', integer)],
  ),
);
```

`listSchema` emits the canonical envelope — `items` (array of the item schema) and `total` (integer), both `required`, `additionalProperties: false` — so `validate`/`require`/`requireMap` and the OpenAPI walker treat it exactly like a hand-written envelope Schema would. `total` is the total matching count across every page, not the page size. Pagination clamping stays a template you copy per handler, not a `c.query` API — `limit`/`offset` reads and their bounds are deliberately not centralized.

## OpenAPI

Attach `doc: RouteDoc(success: Success(status: 200, schema: schema, contentType: 'application/json'), requestBody: schema, requestBodyType: 'application/json', summary: ..., description: ..., tags: ['users'], operationId: 'getUser', failureResponses: {code: schema_or_Failure}, security: [...], query: [...])` to a route. `success` is required: a doc with no 2xx/3xx is unrepresentable, so the emitter never guesses (`POST` answers 201, `DELETE` answers 204 — declare it, don't let it default to 200). `Success.schema` is `null` when there's nothing to document (a 204, a `text/plain` probe; a schema on a 204/304 is an emit-time error). `description` (prose) and `tags` (grouping, aggregated sorted+deduped into the document's top-level `tags`) are optional; `operationId` is optional but **document-wide-unique** — two routes with the same id is a hard error at emit, naming both. `failureResponses` keys must be 400–599 (a 2xx/3xx there is an emit-time error naming the route); each value is a bare `Schema` (defaults to `application/json`) or a `Failure(schema, {contentType: 'application/json'})` for a non-JSON error body (`text/plain`, `application/problem+json`). `query: [QueryParam(name, capture, {required: false})]` reuses `Capture` for its `schema` fragment (projected to `in: query`); required-ness is enforced by the handler's accessor choice (`c.query` vs `c.tryQuery`), not by this flag. A WebSocket route documents its 101 with `doc: RouteDoc.upgrade(upgrade: SwitchingProtocols(subprotocol: ...))` instead of a `success` (see the SSE & WebSocket section).

Validation has two postures and never a third: a malformed *schema* fragment (an `items` that isn't an object, a `$ref` missing from `deps`, a misspelled `type`) is the schema author's defect — a `StateError` that becomes a **500**, never a client violation; only invalid *instance* data (a missing required property, a wrong type, an out-of-set enum value) is a **400**. (`schema.validate` returns the violations; `schema.require`/`requireMap` throw the 400.)

Security is declared, never inferred from middleware. `RouteDoc.security: [bearer]` (an OR-list of schemes; provided constants `bearer`, `apiKey`) overrides the global default passed to `OpenApi.fromRoutes(security: [...])`; `null` follows that default, `[]` declares the route explicitly public. A route with declared security gains an automatic `401` response unless you documented one yourself. The runtime counterpart is opt-in: build one `SecurityPolicy(defaults: [...], verifiers: {schemeName: (c) => bool_or_Future<bool>})` and wire it once, upstream, with `app.use(enforceSecurity(policy))`. It reads the matched route's security off `c.routeDoc` (falling back to `policy.defaults` when the route declares none), OR-combines the required schemes' verifiers, and throws `Unauthorized` when none pass; an empty list (explicitly public) skips the check. A verifier may `c.set` a principal `Key` as a side effect on success — authorization (roles) stays ordinary middleware, not the gate's job.

Emit the document (the code's shadow):

```dart
final spec = OpenApi.fromRoutes(app.routes, title: 'API', version: '0.1.0');
stdout.write(spec.toYaml()); // or spec.toJson()
```

## SSE & WebSocket

SSE: `SseEvent(data, {String? event, String? id, Duration? retry})` is a value type — `data` is the only required field and MAY contain newlines (rendered as one `data:` line per segment; CR/CRLF normalize to LF). The other fields are single-line by construction, so the constructor throws `ArgumentError` on a CR or LF in `event`/`id`, a NUL (U+0000) in `id`, or a negative `retry` (each would forge a second event or silently void the field). `c.sse(Stream<SseEvent> events, {Duration? keepAlive, Map<String, List<String>>? headers})` returns a `200` `Response` with a `Stream<List<int>>` body; it sets `content-type: text/event-stream; charset=utf-8` and `cache-control: no-cache` (your `headers` merge over and may override). `keepAlive` is opt-in — null means no timer is ever created; when set, a `: keep-alive` comment is emitted after that idle interval and re-armed on each event. The subscription and timer tear down on stream completion/error, client disconnect, or `c.aborted` (timeout/disconnect) — whichever first. No new transport: the H1 transport frames the stream as chunked, and `gzip()`/`etag()` pass stream bodies through untouched, so SSE composes unchanged. `SseEvent.toWire()` / `.encode()` give the wire text / UTF-8 bytes if you assemble a body yourself.

WebSocket (over HTTP/1.1): a handshake is a GET — no new verb — so an ordinary `app.get(path, (c) => Response.upgrade(onConnected, {String? subprotocol}))` declares it. `Response.upgrade` carries an `Upgrade` value that fixes status 101 and no body; because the intent to upgrade is a *returned value*, every declaration-driven middleware runs in front of it — a security verifier throws `Unauthorized`/401 and the `Upgrade` is never even constructed. `onConnected(UpgradedChannel channel)` is invoked once by a transport that can switch protocols (inert data otherwise). `UpgradedChannel`: `Stream<Object> get messages` (each a `String` text or `List<int>` binary frame), `void send(Object message)` (String → text, List<int> → binary; a send after close is a state error), `Future<void> close([int? code, String? reason])` (idempotent), `Future<void> get done`. A `subprotocol`, when set, must have been offered by the client or the handshake fails. A non-upgrade request to an upgrade route gets `426 Upgrade Required`; graceful shutdown closes open sockets with 1001. OpenAPI shadow: `RouteDoc.upgrade(upgrade: SwitchingProtocols(subprotocol: ...))` documents the 101 — a `SwitchingProtocols` sits parallel to `Success` and is mutually exclusive with it (its 101 carries no body). Socket-free test: `await TestClient(app, env).connect(path, {headers})` returns a `TestUpgrade` — `.upgraded`/`.socket` (a `TestSocket` with `messages`, `send`, `close`, `done`) when it upgraded, else `.rejection` (the `TestResponse` the pipeline produced instead, e.g. a 401 from the gate).

`timeout(Duration)` never bounds an SSE or upgrade response's lifetime — both are produced synchronously, so the timer never arms; it bounds time-to-response only, and streams must bound themselves (`c.aborted` still fires on client disconnect). `accessLog()` marks such a response with `upgrade: true` / `streaming: true` in its log fields (`ms` is time-to-first-byte, not stream duration).

## Database

`c.env.db.reader` / `.writer` expose `query(sql, [params])` (rows as column-name maps) and `execute(sql, [params])` (affected rows) — both engines take the same `?` positional placeholder (keta_sqlite hands it straight to sqlite3; keta_rds's driver desugars it to PostgreSQL's `$1` form), so one SQL string runs unchanged on either. Type contract: integers `int`, floats `double`, booleans `bool`, timestamps ISO 8601 strings, numeric/decimal as `String`.

Wrap writes in a transaction with the `tx()` middleware and read the connection via `c.get(txConn)`; a thrown error rolls back. `tx()` must be registered *after* (inside of) `recover()` — `app..use(recover())..use(tx())` — never outside it: registered outside, a thrown error is converted to an ordinary `Response` by `recover()` before it ever reaches `tx()`, so the transaction sees a clean return and commits the writes of a request that actually failed; the `keta_tx_outside_recover` lint catches the reversed order.

`keta_rds` is the PostgreSQL adapter: it delegates the wire protocol to `package:postgres`, owns a bounded connection pool (`Unavailable`/503 on exhaustion instead of an unbounded wait), translates a unique-violation (SQLSTATE 23505) into `Conflict`/409 — the floor every `DbConn` adapter honours; an adapter may translate more, never less — and, like keta_sqlite, keeps `numeric`/`decimal` as `String` so a decimal's exact precision survives. Temporal values are mapped to say only what the column type carries: `timestamptz` → UTC with a `Z`, `timestamp` (without time zone) → the ISO string with no offset designator (keta invents no `Z`/`+00:00` the column never held — use `timestamptz` if you want one), `date` → `yyyy-MM-dd`. Connect with `RdsDb.url('postgres://...')` (or `RdsDb(Endpoint(...))`); `dart run keta_rds:migrate` applies `migrations/*.sql` in order (single-applier — coordinate externally in a multi-node deploy), and `db.verifyMigrations(dir)` is the read-only, safe-to-run-repeatedly check each node makes at boot.

## Testing

`TestClient`, `testContext`, `testBothModes`, `TestUpgrade`, and `TestSocket`
all live in `package:keta/test.dart` — a separate import alongside
`package:keta/keta.dart`, not re-exported by it; omit it and `TestClient` is an
undefined name.

`TestClient(app, env)` runs the full pipeline with no sockets: `get/post/put/
delete/patch/head/options`, returning a `TestResponse` with `status`,
`headers`, `text()`, `json()`. `testContext(env, ...)` builds a `Context` for a single handler.
`testBothModes(desc, (mode) { ... mode.wrap(run) ... })` runs an expectation
against both the synchronous-throw and rejected-Future failure shapes.

Contract-green means the *shape* conforms (round-trips, schema validation,
response conformance, invalid body → 400) — not that behavior is correct.
Business correctness lives in `examples` and hand-written tests above that floor.
